text:
Most GenAI pilots I see stalling aren't failing because the model is wrong. The PM work underneath is wrong.

The foundation model is the easy part now. Swap one for another in an afternoon. The hard part is everything most teams skip: which workflow the feature lives inside, what context gets passed in (and what stays out), how the legacy API behaves under load, and whether the pricing model survives contact with inference costs.

Karpathy's framing stuck with me: the context window is RAM, not a hard drive. Teams keep dumping more context in and wondering why quality drops. Precision beats volume, every time.

The other quiet killer is pricing. Flat-rate seats on top of variable token costs is a margin trap. The teams shipping sustainably have moved to consumption pricing for AI features and outcome pricing for agents, with heavier integrations gated behind a premium tier.

The pattern I keep coming back to looks like this:

[see diagram]

The failing 95% aren't losing at AI. They're losing at product fundamentals applied to a new substrate.

Where are you seeing the bigger gap on your team, context design or pricing redesign?

#AIProduct #LLM #SaaS #ProductManagement #AIStrategy #B2BSaaS #GenerativeAI
topic: Building with LLMs in SaaS products

diagram.title: THE LLM FEATURE STACK
diagram.subtitle: What separates the 5% of GenAI features that ship from the 95% that stall.

steps.label: WORKFLOW FIT
steps.description: Pick one workflow with a measurable before/after, not a demo surface.

steps.label: CONTEXT PRECISION
steps.description: Engineer what the model sees; less, sharper context beats more.

steps.label: INTEGRATION DEPTH
steps.description: Wire into legacy APIs with retries, fallbacks, and real auth.

steps.label: EVAL & GUARDRAILS
steps.description: Ship offline evals and production traces before scaling usage.

steps.label: PRICING REDESIGN
steps.description: Match consumption or outcomes to inference cost, not seats.
